intro:
Wisdom (Hebrew chokmah; Greek sophia) is, in biblical thought, the God-given skill of living rightly within the order God has made. It spans the practical and the theological: on one hand the craftsman's expertise (Exodus 31:3), the ruler's discernment (1 Kings 3:28; 1 Kings 4:29–34), and shrewd judgment in the affairs of daily life; on the other, the moral and spiritual orientation captured in the great refrain that "the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom" (Proverbs 1:7; Proverbs 9:10; Job 28:28; Psalm 111:10). These dimensions are inseparable: skill in living is impossible apart from right reverence toward God, for wisdom is finally a matter of the heart aligned with its Maker.

Israel's wisdom literature explores this from several angles. Proverbs distills the moral order into memorable instruction and personifies Wisdom as a woman crying aloud in the streets, offering life to all who heed her (Proverbs 1:20–33; Proverbs 8:1–11). Job probes wisdom's limits, confessing that it cannot be mined like silver but belongs to God alone (Job 28:12–28). Ecclesiastes weighs the vanity of life "under the sun" yet still commends the fear of God (Ecclesiastes 12:13). Solomon, who asked not for riches but for "an understanding mind" to govern God's people (1 Kings 3:9), became the tradition's paradigm — though his later folly shows that wisdom must be continually received, not merely possessed.

The richest passage is the song of Wisdom present with God before creation, "brought forth" as the first of his works and rejoicing as "a master workman" at his side (Proverbs 8:22–31). This became the chief Old Testament soil for New Testament reflection on Christ. The Gospels present Jesus as a teacher of wisdom who declares himself "greater than Solomon" (Matthew 12:42), and the wisdom-and-creation language is taken up to describe him as the one through whom all things were made and in whom they hold together (John 1:1–3; Colossians 1:15–17; Hebrews 1:2–3). Paul makes the identification explicit: Christ "became to us wisdom from God" (1 Corinthians 1:30), the one in whom "are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge" (Colossians 2:3).

Strikingly, the gospel redefines wisdom itself. The message of "Christ crucified" is folly to the world yet is "the wisdom of God", for God chose what the world deems foolish to shame the wise (1 Corinthians 1:18–25). True wisdom is therefore not mere cleverness but is moral and heavenly in character: it "comes down from above" and is "pure, peaceable, gentle... full of mercy and good fruits", over against an "earthly, unspiritual" wisdom marked by envy and strife (James 3:13–18). Such wisdom is given freely to those who ask in faith (James 1:5) and supremely in Christ and by his Spirit (Ephesians 1:17). Wisdom thus runs the whole arc of Scripture — from the fear of the LORD that begins it, to the crucified and risen Lord who is its fullness, in whom the believer is at last made wise unto salvation (2 Timothy 3:15).
